title: What Is a Person? Rethinking Humanity, Social Life, and the Moral Good from the Person Up By Christian Smith The University of Chicago Press. 2011. 528 pages. $40 cloth
abstract: With this book, Smith has made a seminal contribution to the field of work for social scientists who want, on the one hand, to insulate the realm of the human from the materialist reductionism of the evolutionary biological and psychological sciences and, on the other, to give up the obfuscating nonconcepts that are today almost always used in idealist arguments. The tendency in much of contemporary American sociology to imagine human endeavor as mostly or wholly inexplicable by reference to its material level has nearly always had its origins in a fundamentally religious notion of human exceptionalism, but these origins are usually carefully hidden away. Going forward, as it becomes harder for those who want to escape materialism to find an audience willing to accept their increasingly feeble attempts at a compromise position, the consistent among them will perhaps follow Smith, who has here produced a book on social theory that wears its theism unapologetically on its sleeve.
